quiz_questions.question_text: A recent graduate's starting salary is $45,000. Monthly gross pay = $45,000 / 12 =
quiz_questions.explanation: Monthly gross pay = Annual salary / 12 = $45,000 / 12 = $3,750 before any deductions for taxes, insurance, or retirement contributions.

quiz_questions.question_text: With an estimated 25% combined effective tax rate (federal + state + FICA), monthly take-home pay is approximately:
quiz_questions.explanation: Net pay = $3,750 × (1 - 0.25) = $3,750 × 0.75 = $2,813/month. Understanding the gap between gross and net pay is critical for realistic budgeting.

quiz_questions.question_text: Using the 50/30/20 rule on $2,813 take-home: the minimum savings/debt allocation (20%) is:
quiz_questions.explanation: 20% of $2,813 = $563/month for savings and debt payoff. Needs (50%) = $1,407 and wants (30%) = $844 round out the budget.

quiz_questions.question_text: $28,000 in student loans at 5.5% with standard 10-year repayment. Monthly payment is approximately:
quiz_questions.explanation: Using the PMT formula with PV = $28,000, r = 5.5%/12, n = 120 months, the monthly payment is approximately $304. This is a standard 10-year federal student loan repayment.

quiz_questions.question_text: Total interest paid over the 10-year student loan: ($304 × 120) - $28,000 =
quiz_questions.explanation: Total payments = $304 × 120 = $36,480. Interest = $36,480 - $28,000 = $8,480. You pay about 30% extra in interest — motivation to make extra payments when possible.

quiz_questions.question_text: After the student loan payment ($304), the remaining savings/debt budget is $563 - $304 =
quiz_questions.explanation: $563 - $304 = $259/month remaining for retirement savings and emergency fund building. Every dollar of this matters when starting a career.

quiz_questions.question_text: The employer offers a 401(k) with 50% match up to 6% of salary. At a $45,000 salary, 6% contribution = $2,700/year ($225/month). The employer adds:
quiz_questions.explanation: Employer match = 50% × $2,700 = $1,350/year. Total going into the 401(k) = $2,700 + $1,350 = $4,050/year. The match is an instant 50% return on your contribution.

quiz_questions.question_text: The employer match ($1,350 on a $2,700 contribution) represents an instant guaranteed return of:
quiz_questions.explanation: $1,350 / $2,700 = 50% instant, risk-free return on your contribution. No investment in the world guarantees 50% — always contribute at least enough to capture the full employer match.

quiz_questions.question_text: After the 401(k) contribution ($225/month), the remaining amount ($259 - $225 = $34/month) should go to:

options.text: Building an emergency fund first
Individual stock trading
A vacation fund
A car upgrade

quiz_questions.explanation: An emergency fund is the top priority before other savings goals. At $34/month, reaching a $12,000-$18,000 target takes time — this also highlights why increasing income (raises, side work) accelerates financial progress.

quiz_questions.question_text: Renter's insurance at approximately $20/month provides:

options.text: Essential coverage for $20,000+ in personal property, liability, and additional living expenses for just $240/year
Overly expensive coverage for minimal protection
Only covers the building structure
Unnecessary coverage — renters do not need insurance

quiz_questions.explanation: Renter's insurance is one of the best values in personal finance: ~$20/month covers your belongings (fire, theft), liability (someone injured in your apartment), and temporary housing if your unit becomes uninhabitable.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a W-2 form?

options.text: A form to open a bank account
A health insurance enrollment document
A retirement account withdrawal form
An annual statement from your employer showing your total wages earned and taxes withheld during the year

quiz_questions.explanation: The W-2 is issued by employers by January 31st each year. It shows total gross wages, federal/state taxes withheld, Social Security and Medicare taxes, and retirement contributions — essential information for filing your tax return.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the difference between gross pay and net pay?

options.text: They are the same thing
Gross pay only applies to hourly workers
Gross pay is your total earnings before deductions; net pay is what you actually receive after taxes, insurance, and other withholdings
Net pay is always higher than gross pay

quiz_questions.explanation: Gross pay is your full salary or wages. Net pay (take-home) is gross minus deductions: federal tax, state tax, FICA (Social Security + Medicare), health insurance, and retirement contributions. Always budget based on net pay.

quiz_questions.question_text: In the 50/30/20 rule, what does the 30% 'wants' category include?

options.text: Rent, utilities, and groceries
Emergency fund contributions
Retirement savings and investments
Discretionary spending: dining out, entertainment, hobbies, subscriptions, and non-essential shopping

quiz_questions.explanation: The 30% wants category covers discretionary expenses — things you enjoy but could live without. This includes restaurants, streaming services, vacations, gym memberships, and hobby spending. It allows lifestyle enjoyment within a structured budget.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a FICO credit score?

options.text: A measure of your total wealth
A government-issued financial grade
Your annual income divided by your debt
A numerical score (300-850) that represents your creditworthiness based on payment history, credit utilization, length of history, credit mix, and new inquiries

quiz_questions.explanation: FICO scores range from 300-850. The five factors: payment history (35%), credit utilization (30%), length of history (15%), credit mix (10%), and new inquiries (10%). Scores above 740 generally qualify for the best rates.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does APR stand for?

options.text: Annual Payment Requirement
Adjusted Principal Reduction
Average Portfolio Return
Annual Percentage Rate — the yearly cost of borrowing money, expressed as a percentage

quiz_questions.explanation: APR (Annual Percentage Rate) is the annual cost of a loan including interest and fees. It allows borrowers to compare loan costs across different lenders. Note: APR differs from APY/EAR, which accounts for compounding.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a high-deductible health plan (HDHP)?

options.text: The most expensive type of health insurance
A plan with no out-of-pocket costs
A health insurance plan with lower premiums but a higher deductible, often paired with a tax-advantaged Health Savings Account (HSA)
A plan that only covers emergency room visits

quiz_questions.explanation: HDHPs trade lower monthly premiums for higher out-of-pocket costs before insurance kicks in. They are often paired with HSAs, which offer triple tax benefits (tax-deductible contributions, tax-free growth, tax-free qualified withdrawals).

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'liquidity' mean in personal finance?

options.text: How much water a company uses in production
How quickly and easily an asset can be converted to cash without significant loss of value
The total amount of cash in the economy
The interest rate on a savings account

quiz_questions.explanation: Liquidity measures how fast you can access your money. Cash and savings accounts are highly liquid (instant access). Real estate and retirement accounts are illiquid (days/weeks to sell, or penalties for early withdrawal).

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the debt-to-income (DTI) ratio?

options.text: Credit card balance divided by credit limit
Monthly debt payments divided by gross monthly income, used by lenders to assess borrowing capacity
Annual income divided by annual expenses
Total debt divided by total assets

quiz_questions.explanation: DTI = Total monthly debt payments / Gross monthly income. Lenders prefer DTI below 36% (ideally under 28% for housing alone). A DTI above 43% typically disqualifies mortgage applicants.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'financially independent' mean?

options.text: Having enough passive income and investment returns to cover all living expenses without needing employment income
Earning a high salary
Not relying on parents for money
Having zero debt

quiz_questions.explanation: Financial independence (FI) is reached when your investment portfolio and passive income generate enough to cover expenses indefinitely. Using the 4% rule, FI requires saving 25 times your annual expenses.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'compound interest working against you' look like in practice?

options.text: Credit card debt growing exponentially when only minimum payments are made — interest charges on interest charges
Receiving employer matching contributions
Dollar-cost averaging into index funds
Earning returns on a savings account

quiz_questions.explanation: When you owe debt, compound interest works against you. A $5,000 credit card balance at 22% with minimum payments takes 20+ years to pay off and costs over $8,000 in interest. The same force that builds wealth in investing destroys it in debt.

quiz_questions.question_text: The graduate's employer offers health insurance at $150/month. This is essential because:

options.text: It is optional and can be skipped to save money
Medical debt is a leading cause of bankruptcy — a single hospital stay can cost $30,000+, making health coverage a financial necessity
It only covers dental and vision
Young people do not get sick or injured

quiz_questions.explanation: Health insurance is a non-negotiable expense. Without coverage, a single accident or illness can generate $50,000+ in medical bills. At $150/month ($1,800/year), employer-sponsored coverage is among the most cost-effective risk protection available.

quiz_questions.question_text: The $225/month 401(k) contribution at 8% for 43 years (age 22 to 65) grows to approximately:
quiz_questions.explanation: FV = $225/month × [(1.00667)^516 - 1] / 0.00667 ≈ $1,000,000+. Total contributions: $225 × 516 = $116,100. Compound interest adds nearly $900,000. Starting early with even modest amounts produces remarkable results.

quiz_questions.question_text: Adding the employer match ($1,350/year at 8% for 43 years) contributes an additional:
quiz_questions.explanation: The employer's $1,350/year match at 8% for 43 years grows to approximately $500,000+. The employer only contributed a total of $58,050 — compound interest multiplied the free money nearly tenfold.

quiz_questions.question_text: Combined retirement estimate: employee's ~$1M + employer match ~$500K =
quiz_questions.explanation: Approximately $1,500,000 total retirement savings from a modest $45,000 starting salary. Total lifetime contributions were only about $174,000 — compound interest contributed over $1.3 million of the final balance.

quiz_questions.question_text: Using the 4% rule, $1.5 million supports annual retirement income of:

options.text: $60,000/year (plus Social Security for a comfortable retirement)

quiz_questions.explanation: $1,500,000 × 0.04 = $60,000/year. Combined with Social Security benefits of roughly $20,000-$30,000/year, total retirement income could reach $80,000-$90,000 — a comfortable retirement from a modest starting salary.

quiz_questions.question_text: The graduate wonders whether to pay extra on student loans (5.5%) or invest more (expected 8% return). The mathematically optimal strategy is:

options.text: Always invest and never make extra loan payments
Always pay extra on loans regardless of rate
Invest extra money since the expected 8% return exceeds the 5.5% loan rate — but ensure an emergency fund exists first
Do neither — spend the extra money

quiz_questions.explanation: When expected investment returns (8%) exceed the loan rate (5.5%), investing the difference creates a net 2.5% annual advantage. However, the 5.5% debt payoff is guaranteed while investment returns are uncertain. Risk tolerance and emergency fund status matter.

quiz_questions.question_text: Buying a reliable used car at $15,000 financed at 5% for 5 years costs ~$283/month. Leasing a similar new car at $250/month for 3 years results in:

options.text: Receiving a discount on the next car
A full refund of all lease payments
Owning the car at the end of the lease
Returning it with nothing — buying costs more monthly but builds equity in the vehicle

quiz_questions.explanation: Leasing looks cheaper monthly ($250 vs $283) but after 3 years you have nothing. Buying for 5 years at $283/month leaves you with a car worth perhaps $7,000-$9,000 and no more payments. Long-term, buying almost always wins financially.

quiz_questions.question_text: To build a strong credit score, the graduate should:

options.text: Apply for as many credit cards as possible
Avoid all credit entirely
Pay all bills on time, keep credit utilization below 30%, and maintain the oldest accounts
Close all credit accounts to avoid temptation

quiz_questions.explanation: The three most impactful credit actions: 1) Pay every bill on time (35% of score), 2) Keep credit card balances below 30% of limits (30% of score), 3) Keep old accounts open for length of history (15%). A strong score saves thousands on future mortgage rates.

quiz_questions.question_text: This scenario demonstrates that a $45,000 salary with proper financial planning can lead to:

options.text: Nothing significant — the salary is too low
Millionaire status by retirement through consistent saving, employer matching, and compound interest
Guaranteed financial stress
Immediate wealth and luxury

quiz_questions.explanation: A $45,000 salary with disciplined 50/30/20 budgeting, employer match capture, and 43 years of compounding can produce $1.5 million+ by retirement. Financial literacy — not income level — is the primary determinant of long-term wealth.

quiz_questions.question_text: The most important takeaway from this entire financial math course is:

options.text: Start early, be consistent, understand the math behind your decisions, and let compound interest work for you over decades
Memorize every formula perfectly
Avoid all financial decisions until you are an expert
Get rich as quickly as possible through risky investments

quiz_questions.explanation: Financial math is not about perfection — it is about consistently applying sound principles. Start saving early, invest regularly, understand compound interest, manage debt wisely, and make informed decisions. These habits transform modest incomes into financial security.
